turns.content: Is the Mercator projection really that misleading? Show me how wrong our mental model of country sizes is.

Yes—Mercator is extremely misleading for area, even though it’s excellent for preserving local angles (conformality). The “mind-blown” part is that the distortion isn’t subtle: it grows with latitude, and it grows fast. Here’s the key fact: on a Mercator map, the linear scale factor at latitude φ is sec(φ) = 1 / cos(φ). Area distortion is the square of that: sec²(φ). So: - At 0° (equator): sec²(0) = 1 → true area. - At 45°: cos=0.707 → sec² ≈ (1.414)² = 2 → everything looks ~2× too big in area. - At 60°: cos=0.5 → sec² = 4 → ~4× too big. - At 75°: cos≈0.259 → sec²≈14.9 → ~15× too big. - At 80°: cos≈0.174 → sec²≈33 → ~33× too big. That’s why Greenland looks comparable to Africa. In reality: - Africa ≈ 30.37 million km² - Greenland ≈ 2.17 million km² Africa is about 14× larger, yet Mercator visually compresses that difference because Greenland is mostly above ~60°N where area blow-up is huge. If you want a quick “mental model correction,” pick pairs: - Alaska vs. Brazil: Mercator makes Alaska look enormous; Brazil (~8.5M km²) dwarfs Alaska (~1.7M km²). - Europe vs. Africa: Africa is about 3× Europe in area, but the Mercator makes Europe loom.
I want the “why” to feel inevitable. Like, if I knew the math I’d never unsee it. Can you derive the distortion in a way that clicks?

Totally. The inevitability comes from two constraints Mercator insists on, and the geometry of a sphere won’t let you keep them without paying a price. Start with the sphere (or globe) with latitude φ and longitude λ. On the sphere of radius R: - A tiny change in longitude dλ corresponds to an east–west distance: ds_EW = R cos(φ) dλ (because circles of latitude shrink by cos(φ)) - A tiny change in latitude dφ corresponds to a north–south distance: ds_NS = R dφ Now map this to a flat map with coordinates (x, y). Mercator wants to be conformal: preserve angles locally. A standard way to ensure that is to make the scale factor the same in every direction at a point. That means the map’s scale in the east–west direction must match the scale in the north–south direction at each latitude. Let’s define the map: - x = R λ (up to a constant scale; this is the usual choice) Then a small change dλ maps to dx = R dλ. Compare to the true distance ds_EW = R cos(φ) dλ. So the east–west scale factor k_EW is: k_EW = dx / ds_EW = (R dλ) / (R cos(φ) dλ) = 1 / cos(φ) = sec(φ) To be conformal, the north–south scale factor must be the same: k_NS = dy / ds_NS must equal sec(φ). But ds_NS = R dφ, so we need: dy / (R dφ) = sec(φ) => dy = R sec(φ) dφ => y = R ∫ sec(φ) dφ And ∫ sec(φ) dφ = ln|sec(φ) + tan(φ)| + C So: y = R ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) )  (equivalent form) That’s the Mercator “stretch” in y. The unavoidable part: because x is linear in λ, and the globe’s horizontal distances shrink by cos(φ), you must inflate the map’s horizontal scale by 1/cos(φ). Conformality forces you to inflate vertical scale by the same factor. So you inflate both directions by sec(φ), which means area inflates by sec²(φ). There’s no wiggle room: keep angles + keep meridians vertical and parallels horizontal, and the poles must go to infinity.
The poles go to infinity is such a wild statement. Can you show that from the formula, like explicitly?

Yes—watch what happens as φ approaches ±90°. Using: y = R ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) ) Consider φ → +π/2 (i.e., +90°): π/4 + φ/2 → π/4 + π/4 = π/2 tan(π/2) → ∞ ln(∞) → ∞ So y → +∞. Similarly φ → −π/2: π/4 + φ/2 → π/4 − π/4 = 0 tan(0) = 0 ln(0) → −∞ So y → −∞. So the map can never include the poles at finite y. In practice, web maps cut off around ±85.05113° because that corresponds to a finite y in the commonly used “Web Mercator” tiling scheme (it maps to a square).
Web Mercator always felt like a hack. What’s different about “Web Mercator” vs real Mercator?

“Real” Mercator assumes a sphere of radius R (or sometimes it’s adapted to an ellipsoid carefully). “Web Mercator” (EPSG:3857) is a specific spherical Mercator used by most online maps. Differences: 1) Earth model: - True geodetic coordinates use an ellipsoid (WGS84). - Web Mercator assumes a sphere with radius R = 6378137 m (the WGS84 semi-major axis), but it still treats latitude as if it were spherical, not “geodetic on an ellipsoid.” 2) Latitude input: - On an ellipsoid, the conformal Mercator uses a more complicated formula involving eccentricity to be truly conformal. - Web Mercator uses the spherical formula y = R ln tan(π/4 + φ/2) with φ as the geodetic latitude. That introduces small shape errors (not huge at mid-latitudes, but it’s not exact). 3) Practical tiling: Web Mercator is convenient because x and y become “meters” in a projected plane, and the world fits into a square after clipping at ±85.05113°. That supports a simple quadtree tile pyramid. So it’s not “a hack” so much as “a pragmatic standard”: it keeps the Mercator look/behavior that people expect, with computational simplicity and a global tiling system.
I want a mental image for the distortion. Like, if I drew Tissot’s indicatrix on Mercator, what would it do?

On Mercator, Tissot’s indicatrix (infinitesimal circles on the globe mapped onto the projection) stays a circle everywhere—because Mercator is conformal. That’s the twist: it preserves local shapes, but the circles grow with latitude. So the mental image is: same circular little stamp, but its radius increases as sec(φ). Near the equator, tiny circles. At 60°, twice the radius. At 80°, ~5.76× the radius. Since area scales like radius², that’s where the “Greenland ballooning” comes from. If you contrast that with an equal-area projection: - The indicatrix area would stay constant everywhere (but the circle would turn into an ellipse, indicating shape distortion). Mercator: circles stay circles, but their size explodes. Equal-area: area stays constant, but shapes shear/squash.
Okay, but people always say “Mercator is bad.” Yet it’s still everywhere. Can you steelman it?

Mercator is “bad” only if you use it for the wrong task. Steelman: 1) Conformal = angles preserved locally If you care about local shape, bearings, and small-scale geometry, conformality is a big deal. It avoids the “squashed countries” look that bothers people in many equal-area projections. 2) Rhumb lines are straight A rhumb line (loxodrome) crosses all meridians at a constant angle—i.e., constant compass heading. On Mercator, rhumb lines are straight segments. That’s historically huge for navigation with a compass: “hold 270°” becomes a straight line on the chart. 3) Ubiquitous slippy-map tiling Web Mercator became the de facto standard for online maps. That’s not because it’s theoretically perfect; it’s because it’s: - mathematically simple, - continuous, - supports efficient tile pyramids, - looks familiar, - keeps north up and local shapes reasonable. 4) For mid-latitudes at neighborhood/city scale, distortion is manageable If you’re looking at a city map around 40°N, area distortion is about sec²(40°) ≈ 1/(0.766)² ≈ 1.70. That’s not trivial, but for many visual tasks it’s acceptable—especially since users are rarely comparing areas across continents in a zoomed-in view. So: it’s a great local-shape, web-friendly, navigation-historical projection that becomes misleading when you treat it like an equal-area world map.
If I wanted to “feel” the wrongness with numbers, can we do a quick set of comparisons? Like, compute how much bigger Canada looks than it should, compared to, say, India?

We can do a “Mercator visual inflation” estimate by looking at the typical latitude bands each occupies, because the distortion varies with latitude. It won’t be exact (countries span latitude ranges), but it’s revealing. Approximate representative latitudes: - India: roughly 8°N to 37°N; “center” maybe ~22°N. - Canada: roughly 42°N to 83°N; “center” maybe ~60°N (depending on how you weight area). Area inflation factor at latitude φ is sec²(φ). India at 22°: cos(22°)=0.927 sec²≈1/(0.927²)=1/0.859≈1.16 Canada at 60°: cos(60°)=0.5 sec²=4 So, per unit true area, Canada is visually inflated ~4/1.16 ≈ 3.45 times relative to India on Mercator, if you compare “typical” parts of each. Now the real areas: - Canada ≈ 9.98 million km² - India ≈ 3.29 million km² Canada is ~3.03× larger in reality. On Mercator, the *visual* ratio could feel like ~3.03 × 3.45 ≈ 10.5×, depending on where your eye weights the map. That’s why high-latitude countries feel absurdly dominant.
That’s the kind of mind-warp I wanted. But equal-area projections always look “weird.” Is there an equal-area projection that still feels familiar?

A few that often feel less alien: 1) Gall–Peters (cylindrical equal-area) It’s equal-area and keeps a rectangular outline like Mercator, but it massively distorts shapes (equatorial regions look stretched vertically; mid-latitudes look squashed). It’s “familiar” in frame but jarring in shape. 2) Equal Earth Designed recently to look aesthetically similar to the Robinson/Winkel Tripel family while being equal-area. It’s one of the best compromises if your goal is “honest area, not too weird.” 3) Mollweide Classic equal-area with an oval boundary. Distortion is noticeable near edges, but area comparisons are great. 4) Goode’s Interrupted Homolosine Equal-area and reduces shape distortion by interrupting oceans. It’s great for thematic maps (like land use, population density) but the interruptions are visually disruptive. If you want “feels like a normal world map” with less area-lie, Equal Earth is a strong candidate.
Can you explain why Winkel Tripel became so popular? It seems like the “default nice world map” in many contexts.

Winkel Tripel is popular because it’s a compromise projection that tries to minimize three kinds of distortion (hence “tripel”): area, direction, and distance—though not perfectly preserving any of them. Mechanically, it’s the arithmetic mean of: - the Aitoff projection (a modified azimuthal projection) - and the equirectangular (plate carrée) projection, with a standard parallel chosen (often ~50°28′) The result: - It avoids the extreme area blow-up of Mercator. - It avoids the heavy shape distortions of many equal-area projections. - It produces a pleasing oval outline without severe interruption. - It keeps continents looking “recognizable” to the broad public. National Geographic adopted a variant of Winkel Tripel (1998), which massively boosted its “defaultness.”
Let’s go deeper: what’s the conceptual taxonomy of projections? I always get lost in “cylindrical,” “conic,” “azimuthal,” and then all the equal-area/conformal/compromise labels.

Think of it as two orthogonal classification axes. Axis 1: Developable surface family (geometric construction intuition) - Cylindrical: imagine wrapping a cylinder around the globe, projecting onto it, then unrolling. Meridians and parallels often become straight lines. Good for equatorial emphasis. Examples: Mercator (conformal), equirectangular, Lambert cylindrical equal-area. - Conic: project onto a cone touching or cutting the globe, then unroll. Often used for mid-latitudes; parallels are arcs, meridians converge. Examples: Lambert conformal conic, Albers equal-area conic. - Azimuthal (planar): project onto a plane touching the globe at a point (or slicing). Great for polar maps or showing distances/directions from a center. Examples: stereographic (conformal), Lambert azimuthal equal-area, azimuthal equidistant. Axis 2: Property preserved (the “math promise”) - Conformal: preserves local angles/shapes (small shapes), but distorts area. - Equal-area: preserves area, distorts shapes. - Equidistant: preserves distances from a point or along certain lines (not everywhere). - Azimuthal (in the property sense): preserves directions (bearings) from a center point. - Compromise: minimizes overall distortion without perfect preservation. So “Mercator” = cylindrical + conformal. “Albers” = conic + equal-area. “Lambert azimuthal equal-area” = azimuthal + equal-area.
Give me a projection “personality test.” Like, if I care about X, I should pick Y. But make it technical, not self-help.

A technical decision guide: If your map’s purpose is… 1) Accurate area comparison (choropleths, density, land cover): - Use equal-area. Global: Equal Earth, Mollweide, Eckert IV/VI. Regional mid-lat: Albers equal-area conic. Local: Lambert azimuthal equal-area (centered appropriately). 2) Local shape/angles (topographic, cadastral, navigation charts): - Use conformal. Global-ish: Mercator (but beware area). Regional mid-lat: Lambert conformal conic. Polar: stereographic. Many national grids: Transverse Mercator (UTM variants). 3) Accurate distances: - If distances from a single point matter: Azimuthal equidistant centered at that point. - If along meridians/parallels: Equidistant cylindrical (but that’s a narrow promise). 4) Directions/bearings: - From a central point: azimuthal projections can preserve azimuths. - For constant compass heading: Mercator makes rhumb lines straight (but not shortest paths). 5) General reference world map: - Compromise: Winkel Tripel, Robinson. - If you also want equal-area: Equal Earth. And a meta-rule: pick the projection that matches the “measurement” your audience will implicitly do with their eyes.
I want to understand transverse Mercator vs Mercator. Is it literally “rotate the cylinder 90 degrees”?

Conceptually, yes: standard Mercator is a cylindrical conformal projection with the cylinder tangent along the equator (normal aspect). Transverse Mercator uses a cylinder tangent along a chosen meridian instead—effectively rotating the cylinder so it “touches” along a north–south line. Consequences: - Standard Mercator: distortion minimal near the equator, worsens toward poles. - Transverse Mercator: distortion minimal along the central meridian, worsens as you move east/west away from it. That’s why Transverse Mercator is great for narrow north–south zones—exactly the logic behind UTM (Universal Transverse Mercator), which divides the world into 6° longitude zones, each with its own central meridian. Mathematically, the ellipsoidal Transverse Mercator is more complex than simple Mercator, but the mental model “rotate the cylinder” is a good starting point.
UTM feels like a grid cheat code. How does it keep distortions small? And what’s with the 0.9996 scale factor?

UTM keeps distortions small via two tactics: 1) Narrow zones Each UTM zone is only 6° of longitude wide. Since Transverse Mercator distortion grows with distance from the central meridian, keeping the width small bounds the distortion. 2) Secant cylinder + scale factor < 1 Instead of the cylinder being tangent (touching along one line), it’s secant (cuts through the ellipsoid), meaning there are two lines (roughly) where the scale is exact (k=1). Along the central meridian, UTM sets k0 = 0.9996—slightly less than 1. Why? - At the central meridian, you slightly shrink. - As you move away, the projection’s natural tendency is to stretch; that stretch eventually brings the scale back up to 1 at two lines within the zone, then beyond that it becomes >1. This distributes distortion more evenly: you don’t have a single line of perfect scale and ever-worsening stretch; you get a small shrink in the middle and modest stretch near the edges. Rule of thumb: within a UTM zone, scale distortion is typically less than about 1 part in 1000 (0.1%) for much of the zone, though it depends on latitude and exact location within the zone.
Let’s talk about “great circles vs rhumb lines.” People mention it like a mic drop. Can you make it vivid without invoking any navigation scenario?

Picture the globe as a sphere with two kinds of “straightness”: - Great circle: the intersection of the sphere with a plane through the sphere’s center. On the sphere, great circles are the geodesics—locally shortest paths. Examples: the equator, and any meridian. - Rhumb line (loxodrome): a curve that crosses every meridian at the same angle. It’s “constant bearing” in the sense of keeping a constant angle to north, but geometrically it spirals toward the poles if the angle isn’t exactly east–west or north–south. Now the mic-drop contrast on Mercator: - Great circles (except equator and meridians) appear as curves that bow. - Rhumb lines appear as straight lines. So Mercator makes a specific family of curves (loxodromes) look straight, even though they are not geodesics and are generally longer than the corresponding great circle arc between the same endpoints. Vivid mental image: on the globe, a rhumb line is like drawing a line on an orange peel while keeping your pen at a constant angle relative to the peel’s “north.” When you flatten the peel in a particular way (Mercator), that spiral straightens out.
Okay, now I want to get nerdy about “angle preservation.” What does conformal mean in terms of the metric? Like, can you write it as a condition?

Conformality means the map preserves angles between curves at a point. In differential geometry terms, the pullback of the Euclidean metric on the map is a scalar multiple of the sphere’s metric at each point. On the sphere in (φ, λ) coordinates, the first fundamental form (metric) is: ds² = R²(dφ² + cos²φ dλ²) On the map with coordinates (x(φ,λ), y(φ,λ)), the induced metric is: ds_map² = dx² + dy² = (x_φ dφ + x_λ dλ)² + (y_φ dφ + y_λ dλ)² Conformal means: ds_map² = k(φ,λ)² * ds² for some scalar k that can vary with position, but not with direction. In other words, locally it’s like a uniform scaling (same in all directions) plus rotation, but no shear. For Mercator specifically, k depends only on latitude and equals sec(φ). That’s why the indicatrices are circles: the metric is multiplied by a scalar.
If conformal maps preserve angles, why do some conformal projections still “look distorted”?

Because “preserving angles locally” doesn’t mean preserving shapes globally. Conformal guarantees: - At any point, the angle between two intersecting curves is correct. - Infinitesimally small shapes are similar to their originals. But as shapes get larger: - Scale changes across the shape, so one side is magnified more than the other. - The shape can expand non-uniformly across its extent (even if locally uniform at each point), producing global distortion. Example: Greenland on Mercator. - Any tiny neighborhood in Greenland is shape-accurate. - But the scale in southern Greenland is smaller than in northern Greenland. So the whole island is “pulled” larger toward the pole. The island’s overall outline looks plausible, but its area is wildly inflated. So conformal is about local geometry, not global fidelity.
I love that. Now, can we flip it: equal-area projections preserve area but distort angles. What does that look like in Tissot terms?

In Tissot’s indicatrix terms: - Equal-area means each infinitesimal circle on the globe maps to an ellipse with the same area everywhere. - The ellipse’s axis ratio tells you how much angular distortion there is (how non-conformal it is). - The ellipse’s orientation tells you the directions of maximum/minimum stretching. So on an equal-area map, the “stamps” don’t grow or shrink (area constant), but they get squashed—sometimes severely—especially toward edges or poles depending on the projection. A vivid example is the Lambert cylindrical equal-area: - Near the equator, indicatrices are close to circles. - Near high latitudes, they become very wide and short ellipses (east–west stretch, north–south compression), keeping area constant but destroying local shape.
Can you give me a “projection that lies in a different way” than Mercator, like one that makes the poles look okay but does something else weird?

Stereographic projection is a great contrast. - It’s conformal (like Mercator), so local angles are preserved. - It’s azimuthal (planar): you project from the sphere onto a plane, often tangent at a pole. If you center it at the North Pole: - The pole itself looks fine (it’s the center). - Meridians are straight rays from the center; parallels are circles. What gets weird: - Distortion explodes as you move away from the center. The “opposite side” of the globe blows up to infinity at the map’s boundary. - So instead of Mercator sending poles to infinity, stereographic sends the antipode of the tangent point to infinity. It “makes the poles look okay” if you center there, but it sacrifices the far side.
How do cartographers quantify “best” compromise? Like, is there an objective function?

Yes—there are multiple objective functions, and “best” depends on what you penalize. Some approaches: 1) Minimize angular distortion (conformality error) Measure deviation from conformality using the axis ratio of Tissot’s indicatrix. A common metric is the maximum of log(a/b) across the map, where a and b are the major/minor axes (scale factors). 2) Minimize areal distortion Measure deviation of area scale from 1, e.g., minimize ∫(A(φ,λ) − 1)² dΩ over the globe, where A is area scale factor and dΩ is spherical area element. 3) Minimize distance distortion Pick a set of control points and minimize RMS error between true great-circle distances and map distances. 4) Multi-criteria compromises Winkel Tripel is essentially a hand-crafted compromise rather than a strict optimization, but modern projections can be derived by explicit optimization: choose a parametric family and fit parameters to minimize a weighted sum of distortions. So yes: you can define a loss function over the sphere and optimize projection parameters, but the “right” loss depends on intended use and aesthetic constraints (like boundary shape).
I’ve heard “authalic” and “conformal latitude.” Those words sound like secret spells. What are they?

They’re “special latitudes” that translate the ellipsoid problem into something more manageable. On an ellipsoid, latitude is tricky because different definitions exist: - Geodetic latitude: what GPS uses; angle between the equatorial plane and the normal to the ellipsoid. - Geocentric latitude: angle from Earth’s center. And projections often want a latitude that makes a certain property easier. Two key ones: 1) Conformal latitude (χ) A transformed latitude used so that when you apply a spherical conformal projection formula, it matches the ellipsoidal conformal behavior. It’s used in ellipsoidal Mercator and related conformal projections. 2) Authalic latitude (β) A transformed latitude used so that areas on the ellipsoid correspond to areas on a sphere (an “authalic sphere” has the same surface area as the ellipsoid). It’s used for equal-area projections on ellipsoids. So “authalic” basically means “area-correct,” and “conformal latitude” means “angle-correct,” but each is a specific mathematical transformation to deal with ellipsoidal geometry.
Can you show me at least one of those formulas so it feels real?

Sure—here’s a commonly used conformal latitude relation for an ellipsoid with eccentricity e. Define: t(φ) = tan(π/4 + φ/2) * [ (1 - e sinφ) / (1 + e sinφ) ]^(e/2) Then the ellipsoidal Mercator y is: y = a ln(t(φ)) where a is the semi-major axis. That looks like the spherical y = R ln tan(π/4 + φ/2), but with the eccentricity correction factor. For authalic latitude, the formula involves integrating the ellipsoid’s area element; it often appears via a function q(φ) and then: sin β = q(φ) / q(π/2) with q depending on e and sinφ in a more complicated way. Even seeing the conformal one makes it clear: the ellipsoid forces extra correction terms; the sphere is the simplified case.
This is so satisfying. Now let’s do something visual in words: describe how continents “move” or “morph” as you animate from Mercator to an equal-area projection.

Imagine you have a rubber sheet world map that continuously reprojects. Starting at Mercator: - High-latitude landmasses are puffed up like they’re filled with air. - The equatorial belt looks relatively “normal” but slightly compressed vertically compared to the inflated top/bottom. As you morph toward an equal-area projection (say Equal Earth): 1) The high latitudes deflate Northern landmasses visibly shrink—especially the far north. The “top” of the map loses that heavy visual weight. 2) The tropics gain presence Equatorial regions expand in perceived importance because they stop being visually dominated by inflated high latitudes. 3) Shapes shear Because you’re enforcing area conservation, some regions must stretch in one direction and compress in another. Coastlines that felt “upright” may tilt or bow. 4) The map boundary changes Mercator’s boundary is a rectangle (if you clip it). Many equal-area world projections have an oval or rounded outline. So the “frame” itself becomes a cue that you’re not looking at a cylinder unrolled. The gut-level effect: Mercator feels like a world pulled upward toward the poles; equal-area feels like the world relaxing back into its true proportions, at the cost of some coastline geometry looking less familiar.
Can you give me a “projection misconception” list? Stuff people think is true but isn’t.

Common misconceptions: 1) “Mercator was designed to make certain regions look big.” Historically, Mercator’s motivation was navigation and conformality/rhumb lines, not propaganda. The size effect is a mathematical consequence. 2) “There is a correct world map.” There isn’t. You can’t flatten a sphere without distortion (Gauss’s Theorema Egregium). “Correct” depends on what you preserve. 3) “Equal-area maps are ‘true’ maps.” They’re true about area, but can be very untrue about shape and direction. They’re honest for area comparisons, not universally superior. 4) “Conformal maps preserve shapes.” They preserve local shapes, not large region shapes. Large shapes can still be globally distorted. 5) “Projection choice doesn’t matter at small scales.” At global and continental scales it matters a lot; even at regional scales it can matter for measurements, buffering, area computations, and visual interpretation. 6) “All web maps are Mercator, so GIS must use Mercator.” Many GIS analyses should be done in appropriate local projections (often equal-area or conformal depending on task), even if display uses Web Mercator.
You mentioned Gauss’s Theorema Egregium. Can you connect it to the impossibility of a perfect flat map in a way I can repeat?

A repeatable version: “The sphere has positive Gaussian curvature; the plane has zero Gaussian curvature. Gauss proved curvature is intrinsic—you can’t change it by bending without stretching. So to flatten a globe onto a plane, you must stretch, tear, or compress somewhere. That ‘somewhere’ is map distortion.” Or even shorter: “Curvature can’t be flattened away without distortion.” That’s the deep reason every projection makes tradeoffs.
I want to geek out on graticules. Why do graticules look so different across projections?

Because graticules (the network of meridians and parallels) are the “signature” of a projection’s coordinate transformation. - In cylindrical projections: Meridians are often vertical straight lines; parallels are horizontal straight lines. The graticule is a rectangular grid (but spacing changes). - In conic projections: Meridians are straight lines converging to a point (the cone’s apex); parallels are arcs of circles centered on that apex. - In azimuthal projections: Meridians are straight lines radiating from the center; parallels are concentric circles (for many common azimuthals). But beyond that geometry family, properties change the spacing: - In Mercator, parallels get farther apart rapidly toward the poles due to y = ln tan(π/4 + φ/2). - In equal-area cylindrical, parallel spacing is chosen to conserve area, so y is proportional to sinφ (for Lambert cylindrical equal-area), producing different spacing. So just by looking at how the grid bends and spaces, you can often identify the projection family and sometimes the exact projection.
Can we do a “spot the projection” game in text? You describe the graticule and distortion vibe, and I guess.

Yes. I’ll describe; you guess; then I’ll confirm. Round 1: - Map boundary is a circle. - Meridians are straight lines radiating from the center. - Parallels are concentric circles. - Distances from the center to any other point are correct. What projection is it?
Azimuthal equidistant?

Correct. Azimuthal equidistant has exactly that property: correct distances from the center, circular boundary if you map a hemisphere, and that classic “spokes and rings” graticule. Round 2: - World map boundary is an oval/rounded rectangle. - Meridians are curved except the central one, which is straight. - Parallels are straight horizontal lines. - It’s a compromise meant to look “nice,” widely used in reference atlases. What is it?
Robinson?

That’s a very good guess, and it fits. Robinson has straight parallels and a central straight meridian, with other meridians curving. Winkel Tripel also has a similar vibe but its parallels are not perfectly straight in the same way; Robinson is the classic “nice compromise” reference projection. I’ll accept Robinson for this description. Round 3: - Cylindrical world map, rectangular boundary. - Parallels are horizontal straight lines; meridians vertical. - Parallels are spaced so that area is preserved; high latitudes look squashed vertically. What is it?
Lambert cylindrical equal-area?
Exactly.
I want a round where the answer is “Sinusoidal,” because I’ve seen it but never understood it.

Round 4 (Sinusoidal): - Map boundary is a kind of “football” shape: it has pointed ends at ±180° longitude on the equator, and it narrows toward the poles. - The central meridian is a straight vertical line. - Parallels are straight horizontal lines. - Other meridians are sinusoidal curves that meet at the poles. - It’s equal-area. What is it?
Sinusoidal projection.
Yes. The key giveaway is “parallels straight, central meridian straight, other meridians sinusoidal curves,” and equal-area with that characteristic outline.
Why are the meridians sinusoidal? Is it literally x proportional to cos(latitude)?

Exactly. In the sinusoidal projection: x = R (λ − λ0) cosφ y = R φ So at higher latitudes, cosφ shrinks, pulling longitudes inward. That’s why the meridians curve inward and meet at the poles. And because the horizontal scale shrinks by cosφ while vertical scale is constant, the area element works out to be preserved. It’s one of the cleanest “see the math in the picture” projections.
I’m curious: equirectangular (plate carrée) is super common in data, but it looks “wrong.” What’s its distortion behavior?

Equirectangular (plate carrée) is: x = R (λ − λ0) y = R (φ − φ0) It’s not conformal, not equal-area, not equidistant globally. It’s basically “treat latitude and longitude as if they were linear Cartesian coordinates,” which is only locally reasonable near the equator or for small extents. Distortions: - East–west distances are exaggerated by 1/cosφ (because on Earth, degrees of longitude shrink by cosφ, but the map keeps them constant). - North–south distances are constant per degree (which is roughly okay on a sphere, less so on ellipsoid). - Area distortion grows like secφ (not squared) times whatever vertical scaling does; on the sphere with equal scaling, the area distortion is secφ (because vertical is correct-ish, horizontal is inflated by secφ). It’s used because it’s simple and makes gridded data easy to store (regular lat/long grids), not because it’s cartographically good.
Wait—why is area distortion secφ instead of sec²φ there?

Because in plate carrée, the vertical scale is constant and matches the spherical north–south scale if y = Rφ. The horizontal scale is inflated by secφ (same as Mercator’s horizontal inflation), but unlike Mercator, the vertical scale is not also inflated. So area distortion is (horizontal scale factor) × (vertical scale factor) = secφ × 1 = secφ. Mercator inflates both horizontal and vertical by secφ, so area distortion becomes sec²φ.
That’s a clean comparison. Can we talk about “datums” without drifting away? I always hear projection vs datum confusion.

Projection and datum are different layers: - Datum: defines the reference shape and size of Earth (ellipsoid) and its alignment in space. Example: WGS84. It tells you what latitude/longitude means physically. - Projection: takes those lat/long coordinates on that ellipsoid/sphere and maps them to a plane (x,y). So you can have: - Same datum, different projections (WGS84 lat/long projected to UTM, Mercator, etc.). - Same projection math but different datums (projecting NAD83 vs WGS84 can shift coordinates slightly). A coordinate reference system (CRS) usually bundles both: datum + projection + units + parameters.
I want to do a thought experiment: if you were forced to make a world map with zero distortion somewhere, where would you “spend” the distortion?

You always have zero distortion at least at a point (or along a line) for most projections, and you choose where that is. Strategies: - Put minimal distortion where you care most: center the map on your region of interest. - Spread distortion evenly: compromise projections try to avoid extreme spikes. - Hide distortion in the ocean: interrupted projections cut oceans so land looks better. Examples: - Azimuthal projections: perfect at center; distortion grows outward. Great if you care about relationships from a center. - Conic projections: perfect along one or two standard parallels; good for mid-latitude belts. - Transverse Mercator: perfect-ish along central meridian; good for narrow zones. So “where to spend distortion” is basically “where to place the line(s)/point(s) of true scale and how to let error grow from there.”
Are there projections that explicitly optimize for landmasses, like weighting land more than ocean?

Yes—at least in spirit, even if not always formalized. There are projections designed to make continents look good, sometimes via interruption or by choosing parameters that reduce distortion over land. - Goode’s Interrupted Homolosine: explicitly interrupts mostly over oceans to reduce land distortion while staying equal-area. - Some optimized projections use numerical methods and can weight regions (e.g., land pixels) more heavily in the objective function so distortion is minimized where land is. Even without explicit land weighting, cartographers choose central meridians, standard parallels, and aspects to keep inhabited/important regions less distorted.
Let’s do a deep dive on “aspect”: normal, transverse, oblique. How does changing aspect change what “looks distorted”?

Aspect is essentially rotating the globe before projecting. - Normal aspect: projection’s “natural” orientation (e.g., Mercator tangent at equator; azimuthal centered at a pole or equator depending). - Transverse: rotate 90° so what was the equator becomes a meridian relative to the projection’s geometry. - Oblique: rotate by an arbitrary angle. Changing aspect changes: 1) Where distortion is minimized If the projection has a line/point of true scale, rotating moves that to a different geographic location. 2) Which features look “straight” Some projections make certain great circles or rhumb lines appear straight depending on aspect. 3) Visual emphasis An oblique aspect can center an ocean or a continent, making the map feel less “Eurocentric” without changing the projection family. Example: Oblique Mercator is used to map regions with a diagonal orientation (like a long mountain chain) so distortion is minimized along that diagonal great-circle-ish line.
I want to understand “oblique Mercator” without drowning. What’s the intuition?

Intuition: regular Mercator is conformal and treats the equator as the line of tangency (where distortion is smallest). Oblique Mercator “tilts” that line of tangency to follow an arbitrary great circle. So instead of minimizing distortion along the equator or a meridian, it minimizes along a chosen oblique line. That’s useful when the region of interest is elongated along a diagonal direction: you align the projection’s “best behavior” with that axis. It’s still Mercator-ish in that it’s conformal and scale grows away from the central line, but the geometry is rotated so the “good line” isn’t a latitude or longitude line.
Back to mind-blown size comparisons. Give me five “pairs” that are famously misread on Mercator, with true area ratios.

Five classic pairs (approximate areas): 1) Greenland vs Africa - Greenland ≈ 2.17 million km² - Africa ≈ 30.37 million km² Africa is ~14× Greenland. 2) Alaska vs Brazil - Alaska ≈ 1.72 million km² - Brazil ≈ 8.52 million km² Brazil is ~5× Alaska. 3) Europe vs South America - Europe ≈ 10.18 million km² - South America ≈ 17.84 million km² South America is ~1.75× Europe. 4) India vs Argentina - India ≈ 3.29 million km² - Argentina ≈ 2.78 million km² India is ~1.18× Argentina, but Mercator often makes Argentina feel bigger because it stretches southern mid/high latitudes. 5) Indonesia vs Greenland - Indonesia ≈ 1.90 million km² - Greenland ≈ 2.17 million km² Greenland is only ~1.14× Indonesia, but Mercator makes Greenland look massively larger.
The Indonesia vs Greenland one is especially destabilizing. Because Indonesia is this huge spread-out equatorial thing and Greenland is one chunk.

Exactly. Two perception traps stack: - Latitude distortion inflates Greenland. - Fragmentation makes Indonesia feel smaller because it’s many islands spread across water; our eyes undervalue the “sum of parts.” If you put both into an equal-area projection and outline total area, Indonesia’s presence becomes much more apparent.
Can we talk about “map reader cognition” a bit? Like, why does a rectangular world map trick us so hard?

A few cognitive reasons: 1) Frame dominance A rectangle suggests uniformity: equal spacing, equal “importance.” When the boundary is a rectangle, viewers assume the top and bottom are as “real” as the middle. 2) Area heuristic We intuitively read area on the page as area in the world unless told otherwise. That’s why areal distortion is so persuasive. 3) Familiarity bias If you grew up seeing Mercator-like maps, your mental baselines are trained to those silhouettes and relative sizes. 4) North-up + top-weighting People visually prioritize the top of an image. When high latitudes are inflated and placed at the top, they get extra perceptual weight. 5) Coastline recognition over metric truth We’re good at recognizing shapes, so conformal projections feel “right” even when their scale is wildly wrong.
Is there a projection that keeps the rectangle but is equal-area and less ugly than Gall–Peters?

If “must be rectangular” is non-negotiable, you’re in cylindrical equal-area territory, and the main degree of freedom is the standard parallel (the latitude where scale is true). Lambert cylindrical equal-area uses: y = R sinφ x = R (λ − λ0) cosφ0 with φ0 the standard parallel. Gall–Peters is a specific case with φ0 = 45°. Different φ0 values shift where distortion is minimized: - If you choose φ0 around 30°–37°, the shape distortion can feel less extreme in some mid-latitude regions, but other regions will look worse. So you can tune it, but the fundamental tradeoff remains: rectangles + equal-area force dramatic shape changes somewhere.
I want a projection that makes the “whole Earth” feel like an object, not a stretched wallpaper. Any suggestions?

Two that often give that “object-like” feel: 1) Orthographic Looks like a globe viewed from space: circular disk, realistic appearance. Not equal-area or conformal, but extremely intuitive visually. 2) Winkel Tripel / Robinson / Equal Earth These give a cohesive “world as a whole” without the wallpaper stretch. Equal Earth if you want area honesty; Winkel Tripel/Robinson if you want a familiar compromise aesthetic. If you want “objectness,” orthographic is the most visceral: it looks like an actual sphere projection.
Orthographic is the one where you can’t see the far side, right?

Right. Orthographic is like parallel projection of the globe onto a plane: you see one hemisphere at a time, with the limb (edge) being a circle. It’s great for a realistic impression but not for showing the entire surface at once.
Can we talk about “interrupted projections” more? They seem like cheating, but also kind of genius.

Interrupted projections are a deliberate choice to allow cuts so you can reduce distortion in regions you care about. The “genius” is that many distortions become severe when you force continuity across the entire globe. By allowing breaks—usually in oceans—you can: - keep landmasses closer to their true shapes, - avoid extreme stretching near the edges, - preserve area (in equal-area interrupted projections) while reducing shape distortion. The “cheating” is that: - you lose the ability to trace continuous paths across the cuts, - oceans become fragmented, - it can mislead about connectivity. They’re especially powerful for thematic maps where land patterns matter more than ocean continuity.
I want a really technical aside: how do you compute the scale factors a and b for Tissot’s indicatrix from a projection?

You compute them from the Jacobian of the projection and the source metric. In general, you have a mapping from (φ, λ) to (x, y). Compute partial derivatives: x_φ, x_λ, y_φ, y_λ Form the Jacobian J: J = [ x_φ  x_λ y_φ  y_λ ] But because (φ, λ) aren’t orthonormal coordinates on the sphere, you also account for the sphere’s metric: ds² = R²(dφ² + cos²φ dλ²) One approach: - Convert differential vector in parameter space to physical vector on the sphere by scaling longitude component by cosφ. - Define a local orthonormal basis: (dφ) corresponds to R in north–south; (dλ) corresponds to R cosφ east–west. Then the effective linear mapping from local orthonormal basis to the map is: A = [ x_φ/R      x_λ/(R cosφ) y_φ/R      y_λ/(R cosφ) ] Then compute the singular values of A. Those singular values are the principal scale factors a and b (maximum and minimum stretching). Their product ab is the area scale factor; their ratio a/b indicates conformality distortion. For conformal projections, a=b=k.
That’s exactly the kind of thing I wanted. Can you apply it quickly to Mercator to show a=b=secφ?

For spherical Mercator: x = R λ y = R ln tan(π/4 + φ/2) Compute derivatives: x_φ = 0 x_λ = R y_φ = R secφ y_λ = 0 Now form A: A = [ x_φ/R      x_λ/(R cosφ) y_φ/R      y_λ/(R cosφ) ] = [ 0           R/(R cosφ) secφ        0 ] = [ 0           secφ secφ        0 ] The singular values of this matrix are both secφ (it’s essentially a rotation + uniform scaling). So a=b=secφ. Then area scale = ab = sec²φ. Exactly.
I’m now thinking about how projections affect “straight lines.” On Mercator, rhumb lines are straight. On gnomonic, great circles are straight. Can you compare these two in a clean way?

Clean comparison: - Mercator: conformal cylindrical projection. Property: rhumb lines (constant angle to meridians) map to straight lines. Great circles generally map to curves. - Gnomonic: azimuthal projection from the center of the sphere onto a tangent plane. Property: all great circles map to straight lines. Rhumb lines map to curves (often complicated). So they “straighten” different families: - Mercator straightens “constant direction relative to north.” - Gnomonic straightens “shortest-path geometry on the sphere.” Both are useful depending on what you want “straight” to mean.
Does gnomonic blow up too?

Yes. Gnomonic can only show less than a hemisphere at a time. As you approach 90° from the center point, projected points go to infinity. The boundary of the visible hemisphere maps to infinity, so in practice maps are clipped well before that.
I want you to describe what a gnomonic graticule looks like. I’ve never seen one clearly.

On a gnomonic projection centered at some point: - Great circles become straight lines. - Meridians and parallels are generally curves unless the center is at a pole or equator and you’re aligned with them. If centered on the North Pole: - Meridians are straight rays from the center (because meridians are great circles through the pole). - Parallels are not concentric circles like in stereographic; they become curves that get farther apart rapidly and approach infinity as you near the equator (since the equator is 90° away from the pole). So it has a “spiky” look with rapid expansion.
What about stereographic graticule vs gnomonic at the pole? They both have meridians as rays, right?

Right: both have meridians as straight rays when centered at a pole, because meridians are great circles through the pole and both are azimuthal. Difference is in parallels: - Stereographic: parallels are perfect circles, with spacing that increases toward the edge, but the equator is at a finite radius. - Gnomonic: parallels are also circles when centered at the pole? Actually, in gnomonic centered at the pole, parallels project to circles too (because of rotational symmetry), but the radius grows as tan(c), where c is angular distance from the center. So the equator (c=90°) goes to infinite radius. So you never reach it. So both have circular parallels by symmetry, but stereographic keeps the equator finite; gnomonic sends it to infinity.
Nice correction. So stereographic is conformal; gnomonic is great-circle straightening; azimuthal equidistant is distance-from-center. It’s like three different “superpowers.”

Exactly. Azimuthal projections are a neat trio of superpowers: - Stereographic: angles preserved (conformal). - Lambert azimuthal equal-area: areas preserved. - Azimuthal equidistant: distances from center preserved. - Gnomonic: great circles straight. You can’t have all of them at once, so each one picks a power and pays elsewhere.
Lambert azimuthal equal-area: what’s the vibe of its distortion?

Lambert azimuthal equal-area preserves area, so Tissot indicatrices keep constant area, but they become more elliptical away from the center. Vibe: - Near the center, shapes look pretty good. - Toward the edge, shapes get increasingly stretched in one direction, but not in a way that feels as explosively wrong as some projections—because the map remains a circle/ellipse and the distortion is “contained.” It’s often used for hemisphere maps and for regional equal-area mapping because it balances aesthetics and area honesty.
I want to do a purely conceptual exercise: if you could only teach one projection to someone to understand distortion, which one would it be?

Mercator, because it’s the cleanest demonstration of a deep truth: - It preserves angles (so it looks “right” in a seductive way). - Its distortion is mathematically simple (sec²φ for area). - The poles going to infinity is a dramatic, memorable consequence. - It’s common enough that the lesson transfers to real-world map reading. It’s basically the perfect “gateway drug” into understanding that every map is a set of choices.
Now I want to understand “why do some projections have straight parallels?” Is that just a design choice?

It’s often a structural consequence of the projection family and its symmetry assumptions. If a projection is constructed so that: - latitude lines map to lines of constant y (i.e., y = f(φ)) - and longitude lines map based on λ in some symmetric way (often x = g(λ) * h(φ)) then parallels become straight horizontal lines. This happens in many cylindrical and pseudocylindrical projections: - Cylindrical: x depends only on λ; y depends only on φ → both meridians and parallels straight. - Pseudocylindrical: parallels straight; central meridian straight; other meridians curved (x includes cosφ-type factor). It’s a choice that makes the map easier to read and compute, but it also constrains what properties you can preserve.
Pseudocylindrical projections feel like a sweet spot. Are they mostly equal-area?

Many famous pseudocylindricals are equal-area (sinusoidal, Mollweide, Eckert), because the structure lends itself to area preservation: you can control horizontal compression with latitude to match the spherical area element. But not all are equal-area—some are compromise (like Robinson is often categorized as pseudocylindrical-ish in graticule feel even though it’s tabular/compromise rather than a simple formula).
Mollweide: can you explain it without just dumping equations?

Mollweide is an equal-area, pseudocylindrical projection with an elliptical outline. The intuition: - You want the whole world in an oval. - You want equal-area, so the horizontal width at each latitude must be proportional to the cosine-ish behavior of the sphere’s area bands. - Mollweide achieves this by using an auxiliary angle θ that’s related to latitude by solving an equation (because equal-area constraint leads to an implicit relation). Visually: - The central meridian is straight. - Parallels are straight lines. - Meridians are ellipses that meet at the poles. - Distortion is moderate overall, but shapes near the outer edges (near ±180°) get stretched. It’s popular for global thematic maps because area is right and the overall look is clean.
Okay, dump just one equation for Mollweide, because I want the implicit θ thing you mentioned.

Mollweide uses θ defined implicitly by: 2θ + sin(2θ) = π sinφ Then: x = (2√2 R / π) (λ − λ0) cosθ y = √2 R sinθ The “implicit” part is solving for θ given φ (usually done numerically). That’s the price for a nice equal-area oval.
That implicit equation feels like the universe refusing to give us a simple map.

It really does. Many “nice” properties force implicit relationships because you’re matching integrals of the sphere’s geometry. When the constraint is global (like equal-area) and the boundary is aesthetically constrained (like an ellipse), you often end up with an equation that can’t be inverted in elementary functions.
I want to understand “Robinson is tabular.” What does that mean, literally?

It means the projection was originally defined not by a single closed-form formula, but by a table of values giving coordinate multipliers at specific latitudes. For Robinson: - You take latitude φ. - Look up (or interpolate) coefficients that tell you the x scaling and y position at that latitude. - Then x = coefficient_x(φ) * (λ − λ0) - y = coefficient_y(φ) So it’s a designed projection: Robinson tuned the table to make the world “look right” by balancing distortions aesthetically, rather than deriving it from a pure geometric construction. Modern implementations often use polynomial fits to the table, but the origin is tabular.
That’s like hand-tuned distortion, which is kind of poetic.
Exactly. It’s cartography as design: acknowledging that perception and aesthetics matter, not only pure mathematical properties.
I want to do a “projection swap” thought experiment: if all classroom wall maps switched to Equal Earth tomorrow, what misconceptions would fade, and what new ones might appear?

Misconceptions that would fade: - “High-latitude regions are inherently huge.” The inflated dominance of far-north areas would diminish. - “The world’s land is mostly in the north.” It would become clearer how much land area is in the tropics and southern hemisphere. - “Africa is about the size of Greenland/Europe.” Area relationships would recalibrate. New misconceptions that might appear: - People might assume shapes are as reliable as areas. Equal-area maps can subtly distort shapes; viewers might over-trust outlines. - Direction intuition could get worse. On many equal-area projections, straight lines don’t correspond to constant bearings or shortest paths, and direction across the map can be misleading. - The “oval boundary” might make some viewers think the world itself is “oval” or that the edges are less important, affecting how they interpret continuity. Every projection trains a different set of intuitions.
I’m thinking about distortion not just as error, but as a lens. Like, Mercator is a lens that amplifies high latitudes. Can we treat projections as “filters” with predictable biases?

That’s a powerful framing. Projections are filters with systematic biases: - Mercator filter: amplifies scale with latitude, preserving angles. Bias: visually privileges high latitudes; makes shape look trustworthy. - Equal-area filter: preserves area, redistributes shape distortion. Bias: makes density/area comparisons honest; can make familiar outlines look “off,” potentially reducing trust. - Azimuthal centered filter: makes center relationships (direction, distance, or area depending on type) privileged; edges become distorted. Bias: centrality becomes a narrative. - Interrupted filter: privileges chosen regions (often land) and de-emphasizes continuity (often oceans). Bias: emphasizes thematic land patterns. So yes: each projection is a deliberate “visual model” with known transfer functions (scale factors, distortion fields). If you know the bias, you can interpret maps more critically.
I want to end up being able to look at any map and ask: “What is this projection trying to make easy?” That’s the vibe.

That’s the right question. A practical checklist: 1) What stays straight? - Meridians? Parallels? Great circles? Rhumb lines? Something else? Straightness reveals the construction. 2) What’s preserved? - Does it look like local shapes are consistent? (conformal) - Do areas feel plausible globally? (equal-area) - Is there a special center with radial symmetry? (azimuthal family) 3) Where is distortion minimized? - Along the equator? A central meridian? Standard parallels? The center point? Look for spacing patterns. 4) What did the designer prioritize? - Visual familiarity, measurement accuracy, thematic honesty, or computational convenience? With that lens, you’re not just identifying projections—you’re reading their intent.
Keep going. I want more turns, more nerdy tangents, more “I can’t unsee it.” Let’s talk about “scale bars” on world maps. Are they basically lies?

On most world maps, a single scale bar is at best locally true and globally misleading, because scale varies with position and direction. - On conformal projections like Mercator, scale at a point is the same in all directions, but it still varies with latitude. A scale bar placed at 0° latitude is wrong at 60°. - On many other projections, scale varies with both position and direction (anisotropic). So a single bar can’t represent “the” scale. Better practices: - Provide a statement like “scale varies across the map.” - Use graticule and/or a scale factor diagram. - For some projections, provide scale along specific lines (e.g., along standard parallels where scale is true). So yes: global scale bars are usually a polite fiction.
That’s hilarious. So when I see a world map with a scale like “1:100,000,000,” it’s basically a vibe, not a measurement.
Exactly. It’s a nominal scale—often the scale at a particular latitude or at the map’s center. It might be useful as a rough indicator of overall size, but it’s not a uniform conversion factor.
Are there projections with constant scale in one direction?

Yes. Some projections are equidistant in specific directions or along specific lines. Examples: - Equirectangular: constant north–south scale (on a sphere) and constant east–west scale along the equator. - Azimuthal equidistant: distances from the center are correct along any radial line. - Equidistant conic: distances along meridians are correct, and along standard parallels the scale is correct. But “constant scale everywhere” is impossible on a curved surface mapped to a plane.
I want to understand “loxodrome spirals to the pole.” Can you explain that with a tiny bit of math?

On a sphere, a rhumb line keeps a constant angle α with meridians. In latitude/longitude terms, its differential equation is: dλ/dφ = tanα / cosφ If α is not 0° (due north/south) and not 90° (due east/west), tanα is finite and nonzero. As φ approaches ±90°, cosφ → 0, so dλ/dφ blows up. That means longitude changes faster and faster as you move toward the pole. The curve winds around infinitely many times—i.e., it spirals toward the pole, never reaching it in finite “turning” sense. On Mercator, because y stretches to infinity near the poles, that spiral maps to a straight line that goes upward forever.
That differential equation is such a clean punchline. Okay, what’s the analogous “great circle equation” in lat/long?

A great circle can be represented as the intersection of the sphere with a plane through the origin. In spherical coordinates, one form is: tanφ = A cos(λ − λ0) where A and λ0 depend on the plane’s orientation (equivalently, on the great circle’s pole). Another way: if you know two points, you can compute the great circle normal vector and then derive the path. But the key vibe: great circles are “linear” in 3D (plane intersection), not linear in (φ,λ), which is why they generally look curved on most projections.
I love that: linear in 3D, nonlinear in lat/long. That’s a whole worldview shift.

Exactly. Latitude/longitude are a convenient parameterization, not a flat coordinate system. Many things that are “straight” in the intrinsic geometry become nonlinear in those parameters, and vice versa.
Can we talk about “map projection as complex analysis”? I’ve heard conformal maps relate to analytic functions.

On a plane-to-plane mapping, conformality is tied to complex differentiability (holomorphic functions), satisfying the Cauchy–Riemann equations. For sphere-to-plane, it’s a bit different because the sphere isn’t a plane, but via stereographic projection you can represent the sphere (minus a point) on the complex plane. Then conformal maps of the sphere correspond to Möbius transformations (fractional linear transformations) composed with stereographic projection: z ↦ (az + b)/(cz + d) Stereographic itself is conformal, so it preserves angles from the sphere to the plane. Then Möbius transformations are conformal on the plane, so overall you get conformal transformations on the sphere. Mercator isn’t just a Möbius transform; it’s a different conformal mapping from sphere to a cylinder unrolled. But the broader theme is: conformality is deeply linked to complex-analytic structure when you use the right coordinates.
So stereographic is like the gateway between spherical geometry and complex numbers.
Exactly. Stereographic projection turns circles on the sphere into circles or lines on the plane, preserves angles, and makes many spherical geometry problems feel like complex plane geometry.
Okay, bring it back to practical cartography: why do so many thematic maps use equal-area?

Because thematic maps often encode quantities per unit area (population density, rainfall, vegetation cover). If the projection distorts area, then visual comparisons of shaded regions misrepresent the underlying quantities. A choropleth map on Mercator is particularly dangerous: high-latitude regions get visually enlarged, so the same density appears to “cover more space” on the page, biasing perception. Equal-area projections ensure that: - a region’s visual area is proportional to its real area, so area-weighted visual judgments align with reality.
That makes sense. But what about dot density maps? Do they also need equal-area?

Even more so. In a dot density map, each dot represents a fixed quantity. If area is distorted, dot spacing becomes misleading because the eye interprets dot density per unit map area. Equal-area ensures that: - dots per unit map area correspond to dots per unit real area, so perceived density matches actual density. If you use Mercator, high latitudes inflate, making dots look sparser than they should, which is the opposite of truth for many datasets.

I want to do another game: I describe a map and you tell me what projection it might be. Here’s one: world map, oval boundary, Antarctica looks not too gigantic, Greenland not insane, overall “balanced.” Parallels are curved slightly. What’s likely?

Likely candidates: - Winkel Tripel (very common “balanced” oval, moderate polar exaggeration but not insane). - Robinson (also balanced, though parallels are straight in the classic Robinson; if you see slightly curved parallels, Winkel Tripel is more likely). - Equal Earth (if it’s equal-area and has a distinctive gently wavy outline; parallels are not perfectly straight in the same way). If parallels are noticeably curved and the map is a National Geographic–style reference map, Winkel Tripel is the strongest guess.
Another: circular boundary, continents near the edge look stretched, center looks nice, and all meridians are straight lines radiating out. Projection?

That’s an azimuthal projection. If distances from center seem preserved, azimuthal equidistant. If shapes are preserved near center (angles), stereographic. If areas are preserved, Lambert azimuthal equal-area. Without more cues, I’d say azimuthal equidistant is a common one with that look.
Last one: It’s a rectangle, but Greenland doesn’t explode; instead, high latitudes look squashed vertically. Projection?
Likely a cylindrical equal-area projection (Lambert cylindrical equal-area or a variant like Gall–Peters). The “squashed vertically at high latitudes” is a hallmark of equal-area cylindrical.
I feel like I’m building projection intuition. Now: can you explain why “standard parallels” exist? It sounds like a weird calibration knob.

That’s exactly what it is: a calibration knob. In many projections (especially conic and some cylindrical equal-area), you can choose one or two latitudes where the scale is true (k=1). Those are standard parallels. Why they exist: - Distortion can’t be eliminated everywhere, so you choose where it’s minimized. - If your region of interest lies mostly between two latitudes, you can set two standard parallels around that region to keep distortion low across it. In a secant cone projection: - The cone intersects the globe at two parallels. - Along those parallels, scale is exact. Between them, scale is slightly smaller; outside, slightly larger (or vice versa depending on projection). It’s a way to “spread” distortion rather than concentrating it.
Give me an example of picking standard parallels for a mid-latitude belt map, but keep it abstract (no location talk).

Suppose you’re mapping a region that spans roughly 30° to 50° latitude. In an Albers equal-area conic or Lambert conformal conic, you might choose standard parallels near 33° and 45° (or similar) so that distortion is minimized across the band. The idea is: - Put the lines of true scale inside the region, near its north/south extents. - That keeps maximum distortion within the region small. Exact choices can be optimized by minimizing maximum scale error across the region.
I want to understand “why conic projections are good for mid-latitudes.” Is that just because the cone fits that part of the globe better?

Yes, geometrically and distortion-wise. Mid-latitudes form a band around the globe. A cone can intersect that band along one or two parallels, aligning the projection’s natural geometry with the region’s shape. Because: - In conic projections, parallels are arcs with consistent spacing behavior tailored to mid-latitudes. - Meridians converge, matching the real convergence of meridians on the globe, unlike cylindrical projections where meridians stay parallel. So conic projections “agree” with the globe’s geometry in a mid-latitude band, producing lower distortion than a cylinder would for the same region.
We’ve talked a lot about the sphere. But Earth is an ellipsoid. When does that matter for projection distortion in practice?

It matters when you need accuracy beyond “visual” or when you’re doing measurement/analysis. For display at global scale: - Differences between spherical and ellipsoidal formulas are usually subtle to the eye. For precise geodesy and engineering-scale mapping: - Ellipsoidal vs spherical can matter significantly, especially over large extents or when combining datasets. For Web Mercator: - The spherical assumption introduces positional discrepancies compared to a true ellipsoidal Mercator, but the bigger practical issue for most users is the projection distortion itself, not the ellipsoid approximation. In GIS, the datum/ellipsoid choice affects coordinate transformations and alignment between datasets; projection choice affects distortion and measurement.
Can you explain why Web Mercator uses that specific cutoff latitude 85.05113°? It feels oddly specific.

It’s chosen so that the projected map of the world fits neatly into a square in the projected coordinate system. In spherical Mercator: y = R ln tan(π/4 + φ/2) If you want the maximum y to equal the maximum x extent (which is πR for longitude from −π to +π), you solve: R ln tan(π/4 + φ_max/2) = πR => ln tan(π/4 + φ_max/2) = π => tan(π/4 + φ_max/2) = e^π => π/4 + φ_max/2 = arctan(e^π) => φ_max = 2 arctan(e^π) − π/2 That evaluates to about 85.05113°. This makes the world map in Web Mercator fit into a square of size 2πR by 2πR, which is perfect for tile pyramids.
That derivation is so clean it hurts. Okay, I want to talk about “projection choice in data visualization.” If I’m making a global heatmap on a web map, what’s the best practice?

Best practice: - Do analysis/aggregation in an equal-area projection (or on the sphere/ellipsoid using appropriate area calculations), so your bins/areas represent reality. - Then, if you must display on a Web Mercator basemap for familiarity, be explicit that the visualization is on a distorted display and consider compensating methods (like using equal-area gridding in geographic coordinates). If the display itself is the primary artifact (not just a basemap), consider: - Using an equal-area projection for the visualization layer and a matching basemap, instead of forcing everything onto Web Mercator. A common compromise: - Use Web Mercator for interactive panning/zooming but show global summaries in a separate equal-area view.
But most people don’t want to switch views. Is there a way to show area truth on a Mercator base?

You can mitigate, but you can’t fully fix the perceptual bias of the base projection. Options: 1) Use equal-area symbols If you’re placing proportional symbols, compute symbol sizes based on true area/quantity, not map area. 2) Use hex/binning in an equal-area space Compute bins in an equal-area projection, then project bin boundaries back to Web Mercator for display. The bins will look uneven in Mercator, but their real-world area is consistent. 3) Add reference cues Show a distortion overlay (scale factor contours) or include a small inset equal-area map for context. 4) Avoid choropleths on Mercator for global comparisons If the whole point is global comparison, it’s better to change the projection than to fight it.
Distortion overlay sounds cool. What would that look like?

You can draw isolines of scale factor or area distortion. For Mercator: - Scale factor k = secφ depends only on latitude. So contours of constant k are just parallels (horizontal lines). You could label them: k=1 at 0° k=2 at 60° k=3 at ~70.53° (since secφ=3 -> cosφ=1/3) k=4 at ~75.52° (since cos=0.5 gives k=2; sorry—k=4 implies cos=0.25, φ≈75.52°) And area distortion is k². So you could shade latitude bands: - 0–30°: mild - 30–60°: moderate - 60–75°: severe - 75–85°: extreme That overlay makes the distortion “visible” as a field rather than an abstract warning.
I like that. It turns “Mercator is misleading” into a quantitative texture.
Exactly. Distortion is a spatial field. Once you see it as a field, you stop asking “is this map right?” and start asking “where is it right, and in what sense?”
Can we do one more deep technical thing: show how area element transforms under projection using Jacobian determinant?

Yes. On the sphere, the surface area element in (φ, λ) is: dA_sphere = R² cosφ dφ dλ On the map, the area element is: dA_map = |det J| dφ dλ where J is the Jacobian of (x,y) with respect to (φ,λ): J = [ x_φ  x_λ y_φ  y_λ ] Then the areal scale factor is: S(φ,λ) = dA_map / dA_sphere = |det J| / (R² cosφ) For Mercator: x = Rλ => x_φ=0, x_λ=R y = R ln tan(π/4 + φ/2) => y_φ=R secφ, y_λ=0 det J = (0)(0) − (R)(R secφ) = −R² secφ |det J| = R² secφ So: S = (R² secφ) / (R² cosφ) = secφ / cosφ = 1 / cos²φ = sec²φ which matches the earlier reasoning.
That Jacobian method feels like the “adult version” of the earlier derivation.

It’s the same truth in a more general toolset. The earlier derivation used scale matching and conformality; the Jacobian method works for any projection and gives you a direct handle on area distortion.
I think I’m ready for a final mind-blow: is it possible to make a map projection that preserves both area and angles?

Not for any region of nonzero area on a curved surface like a sphere. The only way to preserve both would be an isometry up to scale (a similarity transformation), which would require the surface to be developable (zero Gaussian curvature), like a plane or cylinder. A sphere has positive curvature, so you can’t map it to a plane while preserving both angles and area everywhere. You can preserve both at a single point (trivially) and approximate both in small regions, but not globally.
So the tradeoff is fundamental, not just a limitation of our cleverness.
Exactly. It’s not that we haven’t found the perfect projection; it’s that the geometry forbids it. Every world map is a negotiated truce with curvature.
I want to keep going until my brain is fully rewired. Tell me about “Dymaxion” and why it feels so different.

The Dymaxion map (Buckminster Fuller) projects the globe onto an icosahedron (a 20-faced polyhedron) and then unfolds it into the plane. Why it feels different: - It’s not a single continuous smooth projection like Mercator; it’s piecewise on triangular faces. - Distortion is distributed differently; no single region is consistently “up top” in the same way. - The unfolding can be arranged to emphasize ocean continuity or land continuity depending on how you cut it. It often feels mind-blowing because it breaks the rectangular/oval world-map frame and the usual “north-up” narrative. It makes the Earth feel like a polyhedral object that can be unfolded in multiple valid ways—like a net.
Is it equal-area or conformal?

The Dymaxion projection is often described as “nearly equal-area” and “nearly conformal” in some regions, but it’s not perfectly either across the whole map. Because it’s polyhedral, each face can be mapped with certain properties, but the seams and the overall unfolding introduce discontinuities. Its main appeal is not a strict metric property; it’s the way it redistributes distortion and breaks familiar biases.
Polyhedral projections seem underrated. Are there others used seriously?

Yes. Polyhedral projections show up in: - global raster data representation (sometimes), - educational and artistic cartography, - certain specialized visualization contexts. Examples include: - Cahill’s butterfly (octahedral), - various cube maps (used in computer graphics for environment mapping), - and other polyhedral equal-area designs. They’re less common in mainstream atlases because seams complicate interpretation, but they’re powerful for reminding you the “single smooth world map” is not the only paradigm.
Cube maps in graphics—that’s like mapping the sphere onto six faces, right?

Right. In computer graphics, a cube map represents directions on a sphere by projecting them onto the faces of a cube. It’s not usually used for geographic mapping with lat/long graticules, but conceptually it’s a polyhedral projection: piecewise mapping with seams along edges. It’s optimized for sampling efficiency and uniform angular resolution compared to some alternatives, not for cartographic properties like equal-area.
I like how “projection” means different things in cartography vs graphics, but the same core idea: flattening a sphere-ish thing.
Exactly. Different goals, same fundamental act: turning directions or surface points on a sphere into coordinates in a flat representation, with inevitable distortion and design choices.
I’m going to ask something philosophical: do you think our mental model of the world’s “shape” is more influenced by projections than by reality?

For most people, yes—because the most frequent contact with “the whole world at once” is through a map projection, not a globe. If the dominant projection inflates some regions and places them at the top of a rectangle, that becomes the default mental template. Globes give a more faithful geometric intuition, but they’re less common in daily use, less convenient for printing, and less integrated into digital interfaces. So projections don’t just represent the world; they train the imagination.
That’s the exact vibe I was hoping for. Okay, last technical request: give me a compact “formula crib sheet” of the projections we discussed, just the core x,y relations for the sphere.

Spherical crib sheet (R = radius, λ longitude, φ latitude, λ0 central meridian): 1) Equirectangular (Plate Carrée): x = R(λ − λ0) y = Rφ 2) Mercator: x = R(λ − λ0) y = R ln tan(π/4 + φ/2) 3) Lambert cylindrical equal-area (standard parallel φ0): x = R(λ − λ0) cosφ0 y = R sinφ / cosφ0  (common form; with φ0=0 gives x=R(λ−λ0), y=R sinφ) 4) Sinusoidal: x = R(λ − λ0) cosφ y = Rφ 5) Mollweide: Solve 2θ + sin(2θ) = π sinφ x = (2√2 R/π)(λ − λ0) cosθ y = √2 R sinθ 6) Stereographic (centered at some point; simplest polar form for North Pole, with colatitude c = π/2 − φ): ρ = 2R tan(c/2) = 2R tan(π/4 − φ/2) x = ρ sin(λ − λ0) y = −ρ cos(λ − λ0)  (orientation conventions vary) 7) Azimuthal equidistant (polar form, with c = π/2 − φ): ρ = Rc x = ρ sin(λ − λ0) y = −ρ cos(λ − λ0) 8) Gnomonic (polar form, c = π/2 − φ): ρ = R tan c x = ρ sin(λ − λ0) y = −ρ cos(λ − λ0) That’s the “core math skeleton.” Many variants exist (different centers/aspects), but these capture the basic behaviors.
Perfect. I feel like I just upgraded my brain’s map renderer.
What will the next supercontinent look like? Is Pangaea Ultima the leading theory?

There isn’t a single “leading” theory so much as a small set of end-member scenarios that different researchers favor depending on what assumptions they make about plate-driving forces, subduction zone longevity, and how mantle convection reorganizes. The three most cited supercontinent futures are: 1) Pangaea Ultima (sometimes called Pangaea Proxima): the Atlantic closes as subduction initiates or becomes dominant along Atlantic margins, bringing the Americas back toward Africa–Europe. 2) Novopangaea: the Atlantic keeps widening while the Pacific closes; the Americas continue moving away from Eurasia–Africa, and Asia/Australia converge toward the Americas as the Pacific is consumed. 3) Amasia: continents converge toward the North Pole; the Arctic becomes the “collection zone,” with the Atlantic and/or Pacific partially closing in ways that drive assembly around high northern latitudes. Which is “most plausible” depends on how you weight present-day kinematics (what plates are doing now) versus the possibility of new subduction initiation (especially around the Atlantic). Today’s most obvious long-lived subduction system is around the Pacific (“Ring of Fire”), which tends to support a Pacific-closure / Novopangaea-style future. But supercontinent cycles involve big reorganizations; the Atlantic could eventually develop robust subduction that reverses its growth. So Pangaea Ultima is plausible, but not inevitable. If you want, I can walk through (a) what we can extrapolate fairly confidently for the next 50–100 million years, (b) what becomes highly contingent after that, and (c) what each scenario implies for geography, climate, and ocean circulation.
Start with what we can extrapolate with some confidence for the next 50–100 million years. I want specifics: which oceans shrink, which grow, what happens to Africa, Australia, Antarctica, etc.

Over ~50–100 million years, the best-constrained part is the continuation of present plate motions and the consumption of existing subduction zones. That said, even within 100 Myr, plate reorganizations can happen, but we can still outline a “most likely” trajectory based on current vectors. Key present-day trends and near-future implications: 1) Pacific Ocean: generally shrinking - The Pacific Plate is being subducted along much of its margins (Aleutians, Japan, Izu-Bonin-Mariana, Tonga-Kermadec, parts of Central/South America). - Spreading centers exist (East Pacific Rise), but the net area of the Pacific basin tends to decrease because subduction “wins” in many reconstructions. - Over tens of millions of years, expect continued closure of parts of the Pacific, especially in the western Pacific where complex plate interactions are actively consuming oceanic lithosphere. 2) Atlantic Ocean: generally widening (for now) - The Mid-Atlantic Ridge continues to produce new seafloor. - There is limited, discontinuous subduction in the Atlantic realm (e.g., the Lesser Antilles arc is subduction of Atlantic lithosphere, and the Scotia arc region is complicated), but nothing comparable to the Pacific’s encircling subduction belts. - Over 50–100 Myr, a reasonable extrapolation is further widening of the Atlantic, pushing the Americas farther from Europe/Africa. 3) Indian Ocean: mixed behavior - The Indian Ocean has spreading ridges, but India’s northward motion has already closed the Tethys and built the Himalaya-Tibet system. - The Indian plate continues to indent Eurasia; convergence may keep thickening and laterally extruding parts of Asia. - The eastern Indian Ocean and SE Asia are extremely complex; subduction around Indonesia likely continues to consume oceanic lithosphere and rearrange microplates. 4) Africa: moving north, colliding with Europe; rifting in East Africa - Africa continues to move generally northward relative to Eurasia, meaning continued convergence across the Mediterranean region. - In 50–100 Myr, much of the Mediterranean could be gone, replaced by a mountain belt akin to a younger Himalaya-style orogen (often called the “Alpine-Himalayan” belt continuing to evolve). - The East African Rift is actively opening; if it continues, it may eventually split off a Somali Plate “microcontinent” and create a new ocean basin (a future “Red Sea on steroids”). - Whether that rift becomes a full ocean within 100 Myr is plausible but not guaranteed; rifts can stall. 5) Australia: moving north toward SE Asia - Australia is currently moving rapidly northward (several cm/yr). - Expect continued collision and subduction interactions with Indonesia and SE Asia; island arcs, accretionary prisms, and mountain building in that region intensify. - Over 50–100 Myr, Australia could be largely sutured to Asia, contributing to a broader Eurasian landmass and altering ocean gateways. 6) Antarctica: relatively isolated, but influenced by surrounding spreading/subduction - Antarctica’s position is relatively stable in many reconstructions, though West Antarctica is tectonically more complex. - The Southern Ocean spreading systems continue; changes in Australia/Antarctica separation rates and in the Scotia region can alter ocean circulation pathways. - Big changes might come indirectly: if Australia fully collides with Asia, ocean circulation between the Indian and Pacific could change; if Africa-Europe closes the Mediterranean, that changes global circulation too. 7) The Americas: west coasts remain active margins - Western North America continues to interact with Pacific plates; subduction persists in Cascadia and Alaska, while transform motion dominates along parts of California. - Central America and the Caribbean remain a complex region of small plates and subduction/collision. - The Andes remain a major subduction-driven mountain belt as long as the Nazca Plate (and successors) continues to subduct.
